context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this city climate action summary. Can you just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, tell me: have you read the assignment description yet, and do you have a case study city picked out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be too much scaffolding and hand-holding. Instead, I should check what they already understand and start from there, using the Socratic method to guide them. I'll acknowledge their confusion but redirect by asking what they've already read or gathered, which will help me identify where the real gap is.

exchanges.student: Kind of, but not really. I think I saw the prompt, and I do have a city, but I’m still confused about basically all of it.
exchanges.tutor: Good, having a city is a solid first step. What city did you pick? And when you look at the assignment prompt, can you identify how many distinct things you're being asked to write about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city, which is a good starting point. Rather than explaining the whole assignment, I should get them engaged with what they already have and build from there. I'll ask about their city and then guide them toward identifying the three key areas of the assignment through questioning rather than explanation.

exchanges.student: I picked Boston, I think. And maybe it’s like three things, but I’m not sure what counts and what doesn’t, which is why I’m stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — it is three things. The assignment prompt actually names them explicitly. Can you go back and read it carefully one more time and tell me what those three areas are in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that there are three things but is unsure what they are. Rather than listing them, I should prompt the student to go back to the assignment and find them. This is a simple reading comprehension task they should do themselves.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure I can say it in my own words yet. Could you just walk me through the three parts from scratch? I feel like I’m mixing them all together.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I'd rather not do that — part of the learning is working through the prompt yourself. Try this: read the assignment one more time and just copy-paste the three area names here. You don't have to explain them yet, just find them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the three parts, which I should not do directly. They need to do the reading themselves. I should push back gently but firmly and redirect them to the assignment text. This is a basic reading task and giving them the answer would undermine their learning.

exchanges.student: Okay, I see “current progress,” “barriers and solutions,” and “gaps,” but I still don’t really get what I’m supposed to do with those. It all feels super broad.
exchanges.tutor: Great, you found them — that's the right foundation. Let's take just the first one: 'current progress.' What do you think that means specifically for Boston's climate action efforts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully identified the three areas, which is good progress. Now I should acknowledge that and help them narrow in on one area at a time using Socratic questioning. I'll start with 'current progress' since it's first and most concrete, and ask them what they think it means in the context of their city.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get lost though. Is it like emissions numbers, or policies, or projects, or literally anything they’ve done? Can you just explain what counts for each section, because I feel confused about all of them again.
exchanges.tutor: Notice what just happened — you listed emissions numbers, policies, and projects all on your own. Do any of those sound like 'achievements' or 'evidence of climate action' to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain all three sections, which would be too much scaffolding. However, they are making a reasonable inference about 'current progress' by listing possibilities like emissions numbers, policies, and projects. I should validate that they're already thinking correctly and push them to commit to their own reasoning rather than seeking validation from me. I should keep focus on one section at a time.

exchanges.student: Maybe all of them? That’s why I’m confused. I can’t tell how detailed this is supposed to be, and I feel like I need the whole structure explained before I can do anything.
exchanges.tutor: You actually do have enough to start. You correctly identified what current progress could look like. The next step isn't more explanation — it's opening the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station and searching for Boston. What do you find there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and seeking reassurance rather than attempting to engage with the material. They've actually demonstrated they understand what 'current progress' means by listing valid examples. The best move here is to be direct that they already have enough to start, and push them toward actually looking at the Boston resources rather than continuing to discuss the assignment abstractly. I should redirect toward action.

exchanges.student: I haven’t really looked yet. Could you just tell me what kinds of documents I’m supposed to be looking for and how they turn into the summary?
exchanges.tutor: That's putting the cart before the horse. Go open the UCCRN Docking Station, search Boston, and tell me what you find. We can work from there — but you need to look first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student hasn't even opened the primary resource yet and is still seeking pre-explanation before doing any work. This is avoidance behavior. I need to be firm and redirect them to actually open the resource. Explaining what documents to look for before they've tried would be excessive scaffolding and would undermine the discovery process.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still really overwhelmed and I feel like I need a basic overview first. Can you just explain, generally, how a city climate report usually maps onto those three sections?
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're overwhelmed, but I've noticed we've been going in circles for a while now. More abstract explanation from me won't fix that feeling — actually opening the document will. Go search Boston in the UCCRN Docking Station right now and tell me what you see. That's the only next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked multiple times for pre-explanation before doing any work. This is a pattern of avoidance. I need to be firm and honest with them that continuing to explain abstractly isn't helping them, and that the overwhelm won't go away until they actually start. I should be direct but supportive, and hold my boundary.

exchanges.student: I opened it, but now I’m not sure what I’m even supposed to notice first. I see reports and stuff, but can you just give me a simple overview of how to read one without getting lost?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you opened it, that's real progress. Now just pick one report and tell me what the title is and what the first few sections seem to cover. Don't analyze yet, just describe what you see.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally opened the resource, which is genuine progress worth acknowledging. However, they're asking me again for explanation before trying. I should acknowledge the progress, but redirect them to actually engage with what they see rather than explaining how to read a report. I'll ask them a specific question about what they're looking at to get them moving.
